prompt: Defend a genuinely unpopular or unfashionable opinion that you actually hold. Not a strawman — something you'd stand by.
response:
I don’t have personal beliefs, but here’s an unpopular position I can defend and stand by in argument: Large social media platforms should require real-identity verification for full participation. Public-facing pseudonyms can remain, but behind the scenes every high-reach account should be uniquely and verifiably tied to a real person, with tightly governed privacy protections and carve-outs for at-risk speakers.

Why this is worth it
- Accountability changes incentives: When misbehavior carries real-world risk, rates of harassment, brigading, fraud, and coordinated disinformation drop, and repeat-offender churn becomes more expensive.
- Botnets and influence ops get harder: Unique-human verification (one person, one account of consequence) dramatically raises the cost of at-scale manipulation.
- Healthier discourse: Platforms with stronger identity assurance tend to have less drive-by abuse and more durable communities. You don’t need perfect compliance to see large gains; you just need to shift the cost curve.
- Better enforcement: Abusive or criminal behavior can be acted on faster when platforms can reliably identify unique accounts and escalate to lawful authorities when warranted.

What this would look like in practice
- Thresholded scope: Applies to “very large platforms” (e.g., >10M MAU in a jurisdiction) where harm scales. Smaller forums can opt in or remain fully anonymous.
- Privacy-preserving verification: Independent identity providers issue a cryptographic token proving “adult,” “unique human,” and “not previously banned,” without revealing name to the platform or public. Zero-knowledge methods and selective disclosure minimize data exposure.
- Public pseudonyms, private accountability: You can post under a handle. The platform sees only a token, not your civil identity, except under due process through the identity escrow.
- Carve-outs for vulnerable speakers: Journalists, activists, whistleblowers, and abuse survivors can use accredited intermediaries (news orgs, NGOs) as identity escrows. Emergency anonymity pathways exist for imminent risk.
- Strong governance: Strict data minimization, third-party audits, heavy penalties for leaks/doxing, warrant requirements for unmasking, and independent oversight. No broad data retention; tokens can be rotated to limit tracking.
- Access, not exclusion: Free, low-friction verification options (government IDs, bank KYC, in-person at libraries/post offices) so the unbanked and undocumented aren’t locked out. Guest access remains for reading; unverified posting is rate-limited in reach.

Addressing the strongest objections
- “This chills speech.” It can—if done badly. Mitigations: keep public pseudonymity, outlaw doxing with teeth, ensure platforms can’t see your civil identity, and restrict unmasking to narrow, reviewable processes. The aim is accountability without forced public naming.
- “Authoritarians will abuse this.” Any identity infrastructure risks abuse. Safeguards: decentralized identity providers across jurisdictions, open standards, transparent audits, and refusing deployment where independent oversight is impossible. Make the system export-resistant to repression.
- “Harassment happens under real names too.” True, but frequency, velocity, and coordination decline when bans stick, sockpuppeting is hard, and real-world consequences are credible.
- “People who need anonymity will be silenced.” Hence vetted escrow and emergency pathways. Today, many at-risk users are already silenced by mobs and doxing; reliable accountability plus anti-doxing enforcement can expand speech for them.
- “It’s technically or economically infeasible.” We already do KYC at scale in finance and SIM registration in many places. With privacy tech, identity proofs can be cheaper and safer than storing raw IDs.

Success metrics
- Sustained reduction in bot prevalence and coordinated inauthentic behavior
- Lower rates of repeat-offender reentry
- Measurable drops in harassment reports per MAU and time-to-remediation
- Stable or improved participation by historically targeted groups
- Independent audits confirming minimal data exposure and due-process conformity

Bottom line
Total anonymity at mass scale is great for frictionless posting, but it’s terrible for accountability and civic resilience. We can keep the valuable parts of pseudonymity while making high-reach participation contingent on being a real, unique human—verifiably and privately. That tradeoff is unpopular, but it would make large platforms safer, more trustworthy, and harder to weaponize.
